I’m following suggestion 1 from <https://mail.jabber.org/pipermail/standards/2017-January/032048.html>:

> Current best practice is for MUC implementations to add a tag to all messages (groupchat and PMs) sent from the MUC:
>
>        <x xmlns="http://jabber.org/protocol/muc#user">
>
> Carbons implementations do check for presence of this tag and disable CC'ing accordingly. We need to codify this, and it belongs into both XEP-0045 (adding the <x/> tag) and into XEP-0280 (using the tag as a filter criterion). I'm willing to create PRs for both once consensus is reached ("45 version bump", anyone?).

I also realise just now that this doesn’t affect us, because we do not "send from the MUC" and are thus not the MUC implementation Georg is talking about there. However, it doesn’t hurt and may in fact help if the MUC we’re using doesn’t follow this suggestion and also does not strip the ``<x/>`` we’re adding.

Avs uses python `timeit` module and some of IPython `%timeit` magic, so I will not consider this reliable/reproducible for timings less than 0.1 seconds or even 1 seconds. [[1]](https://vstinner.github.io/journey-to-stable-benchmark-system.html) [[2]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1602.00602.pdf)

< TLDR >
The TLDR is that you have to select what to measure: average execution time or "platonic" execution time (the fastest and usually not very representative). When you do this you need to understand how timeit and %timeit and other tools are measuring. If your individual executions are fast, there is ton of noise that affects the measurements.
< /TLDR >

If you don't want to limit the noise (which is [very complicated](https://vstinner.github.io/journey-to-stable-benchmark-system.html)), then another approach is to limit/understand the effect of the noise using statistics. As the initial distribution for the time measurements has skewness <0, assuming that the distribution is homeomorphic to `P(X=x)=a e^(-a(x-b)) for X>b`, 0 otherwise (where X is a random variable and b,a are real numbers), then the typical procedure to obtain stable benchmarks by limiting the noise  is measuring execution n times, doing the mean of this values and repeating this r times. Using this fact, the distribution representing the means: `Y=1/n(X1+X2+...)` For the central limit theorem we know that `P(X=Y) ~ N(sigma, mu)` Where N is the normal distribution of stdv sigma and mean mu. This is a profound result and therefore you don't need to worry about the left skewness or higher central momenta of the distribution of errors, the means of the execution times will be always asymptotically a normal distribution. This means that reporting the mean and the standard deviation of a statistically significant number of experiments is enough because there are no higher momenta available. Notice that this is true even if the errors are on one side of the execution result. This is trying to answer another problem that is "what is the most representative execution time in this particular machine" as opposed to "what is the platonic execution time for this set of instructions". IMHO users want reproducibility and some sense of stability, even under noisy and unstable conditions.

I think it also depends on how seriously do you want this to be. A lot of people are using avs because they don't care about the inexactitudes that much, or because they don't know best. Actually, avs is doing a lot of good work to set up the environment and produce proper metrics (you can select warmup_time,repeat,number and sample times among other options) which I think you can use to be very close to stable benchmarking. Another things you probably want to do in the machine you run this is to deactivate frequency switching in the CPU that you are using and isolate one CPU core for running the benchmarks.

Eh I think ``done()`` is a poorly chosen name. Mainly because ``done()`` does not actually mean the transfer is done, as there is nothing left to do. ``done()`` just means that the status has been set to a terminal status, but there could be extra activities happening. The **only** thing that indicates there is nothing left to do in the transfer is the setting of that done event. The main problem is that status and this done event are set independently. In this case, I am using the fact that these are independently set to pretty much determine if it is in a "is shutting down" state.

I think given the time, I would do the following to make this logic more clear:

1) Introduce a "shutting-down" state that if an error or cancel occurred, it would be set and then shifted over to failed and cancelled once done was announced.
2) Move "success" state over from "running" when done is announced, instead of setting it when ``set_result()`` is called.
3) Remove this logic to execute main if it is not shutting down.
4) Abstract all this logic more because it is getting complicated.

Does that make sense? I could make this change but seems outside the scope of this PR, but I'd be happy to make that change. What are your thoughts/suggestions?

1. It prevents us from cluttering the top-level of Python codebase with a "performance" directory

I suggest using http://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.searchsorted.html here.

```python
# get edges into 1D array[L, R, L, R, ...]
all_edges = np.ravel(np.reshape(bin_edges, (2, -1))
assert np.all(np.diff(all_edges) >= 0)
# sort out where the array each time would be inserted
binning = np.searchsorted(all_edges, time)
# times that would get inserted at even positions are between bins, drop them
binning[binning%2 == 0] = 0
```

I think the `searchsorted` method scales as O(n_times * log(n_bins)) where as the implemented method scales an O(n_times * n_bins) and uses a python level loop.

Yes I agree that `PreventRegexInitializer` seems hacky solution and its better to delete it. Having `prevent_regexes: List[str] = None` argument in `InitializerApplicator` and removing `PreventRegexInitializer` seems much cleaner. I will make these changes. Thank you for the suggestions.

However, poping `"prevent_regexes"` key in `from_params` of `InitializerApplicator` is not possible since `params` argument is a List of tuples `List[Tuple[str, Params]]` as following:

```
[
    ["parameter_regex_match1",
        {"type": "normal","mean": 0.01, "std": 0.1}
    ],
    ["parameter_regex_match2", "uniform"]
]
```
How about the following: To add prevent regex we add `(".*_my_prevention_regex_*",  "prevent")` tuples in the above list. And in `from_params`, from `params` list, I extract and remove the tuples with second item "prevent" and make `prevent_regexes` out of first items. And finally initialize `Initializer`s:
```python
def from_params(cls, params: List[Tuple[str, Params]]) -> "InitializerApplicator":
    prevent_regexes = [ param[0] for param in params if param[1] is "prevent"]
    params = [ param for param in params if param[1] is not "prevent"]

    initializers = [(name, Initializer.from_params(init_params, prevent_regexes)) for name, init_params in params]
    return InitializerApplicator(initializers)
```
How does this seem? Please correct me if I misinterpreted your suggestion.

Re: the tokenizer, for the semantic parser you almost certainly want different tokenizers for the input and the output, because the output is probably just space-separated, and trying to split it as if it were English would be wrong. At first I thought this was a neat optimization, but I was concerned that some decompression implementations may observe the truncated IPv6 Option and assume the packet is malformed.  Thanks for updating.

The number of buffers to allocate per instance largely depends on the amount of buffering you want for MTD children.  For non-sleepy MTDs, a router needs to reassemble IPv6 datagrams before delivering them to the MTD.  The more important issue to consider is that for sleepy MTDs, the router must buffer messages until the MTD polls for them.  The poll period is determined by the MTD and could be minutes or more.  To first order, I would size the buffer pool based on the number of sleepy children you wish to support and the amount of buffer per sleepy child.

You are correct that we may have one wasted data poll but it will happen only in the rare scenario from the description (i.e., child gets the "Child ID Response" and parent misses the ack to it). I think that may be fine.

The check above is in addition to the existing code where we switch to using short address after a successful indirect tx to the child. So in the common case where the Child ID Resp. exchange is successful, we won't have any wasted data request.  

I am not sure about switching to short address after first (successful/failed) indirect tx. If we do this, we are basically limiting the  "Child ID response" to a single tx attempt. If the first attempt is unsuccessful, parent switches to using short address while child is still on long address. All later tx attempts fail and child needs to start with a new "Child ID Request".

There is a major issue with ```add_nodes_from``` function. We have not defined a ```add_node``` method in this class, so when someone tries to use call ```add_node()``` method it would be using the ```add_node``` method from ```BayesianModel``` class. For example 

```python
G = DynamicBayesianModel()
G.add_node('A')
```

it won't be adding ```A``` in ```list_of_nodes```, as there is no such variable in ```BayesianModel```. It would lead to inconsistency. So my suggestion is don't define ```add_node``` and ```add_nodes_from``` until you want to have some extra constraints on the nodes. And whenever you want to access the nodes instead of using ```list_of_nodes``` just call ```self.nodes()```.
